             trial.study.stop()

+    if settings.study_checkpoint_file is not None:
+        resume = os.path.exists(settings.study_checkpoint_file)

discussion_comments.comment_body: This is bad UX. If the user says no to resuming the previous study, the program should start a new study, not quit and discard all the progress (e.g. batch size determination) made already.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
That's right - every time the user aborts with Ctrl+C, you get an incomplete trial in `study.trials` which also gets added to the log. I think a crash doesn't do this as it stops before anything is written for the incomplete trial.

Either way, I'm not sure what the best way to solve this is - just `delete` the last trial before resuming if it's incomplete? User aborts should be the only source of them, I think.

Trying to clean up incomplete trials in the middle could get tricky (e.g. having to adjust all subsequent indices) and I'm not sure it's worth it compared to just having a few less results at the end.

discussion_comments.comment_body: I don't think we need to do anything to "fix" incomplete trials. We already ignore them when calculating the Pareto front, and TPESampler ignores incomplete trials as well. We could extend n_trials with the number of incomplete trials so that the number of complete trials eventually matches the desired n_trials.

discussion_comments.comment_body: Hmm, true, but we don't prune trials. We'd have to explicitly call `trial.should_prune()` in the objective function and then `raise optuna.TrialPruned()`, as in the example for [MedianPruner](https://optuna.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/generated/optuna.pruners.MedianPruner.html) (the default pruner).

+            study.optimize(objective_wrapper, n_trials=remaining_trials)
+        elif remaining_trials < 0:
+            settings.n_trials = len(study.trials)
+
     except KeyboardInterrupt:
         # This additional handler takes care of the small chance that KeyboardInterrupt
         # is raised just between trials, which wouldn't be caught by the handler
         # defined in objective_wrapper above.
         pass
 
+    if len(study.trials) == settings.n_trials:

discussion_comments.comment_body:
That just handles the exception if it *is* raised by the objective function - but we never raise it. As for how TPESampler uses pruned trials, I think it just throws all of them into the "bad" distributions.

But since we don't prune, I don't think we have to worry about that. If Heretic ever starts pruning unpromising trials after some intermediate result, it should be easy enough to adjust the logic for it then.

if len(study.trials) == settings.n_trials + extra_trials:

     # Silence the warning about multivariate TPE being experimental.

+    study_checkpoint_file = os.path.join(

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Ah! Indeed, that would set `trial.state` to `TrialState.PRUNED`, then stop right after. But we ignore pruned trials here: https://github.com/anrp/heretic/blob/a68fc361911a7c5b459325c9fffb2d540840cdeb/src/heretic/main.py#L575

I think this is correct, as there is no value in a trial with a state other than `TrialState.COMPLETE`.

user: str
 
 
+def _get_split_slice(split_str: str, length: int, split_name: str) -> tuple[int, int]:
+    """
+    Parse a split specification and return (start, end) indices.
+    Uses the datasets library's ReadInstruction for robust parsing.
+    """
+    instruction = ReadInstruction.from_spec(split_str)
+    name2len = {split_name: length}
+    abs_instruction = instruction.to_absolute(name2len)[0]
+    return abs_instruction.from_, abs_instruction.to
+
+
 def load_prompts(
     settings: Settings,
     specification: DatasetSpecification,
 ) -> list[Prompt]:
     path = specification.dataset

-                "Loading dataset dicts is not supported"
+    # Support for plain text files (one prompt per line)

+            prompts = [line.strip() for line in f if line.strip()]
+
+        # Apply split specification if provided.
+        # We treat the list of prompts as a single split, so the name is arbitrary.
+        if split_str is not None:
+            try:
+                start, end = _get_split_slice(split_str, len(prompts), "_")
+                prompts = prompts[start:end]
+            except (ValueError, IndexError):
+                # If split_str doesn't contain valid slice notation, use all prompts.

+def get_merged_model(model: Model, settings: Settings):
+    """
+    Returns the model with LoRA adapters merged if applicable.
+    For quantized models, prompts the user for how to proceed.
+    Returns None if user chooses to save LoRA adapter only.
+    """
+    if not isinstance(model.model, PeftModel):
+        # No LoRA adapters, return the model as-is
+        return model.model
+
+    # Prompt for all PEFT models to ensure user is aware of merge implications
+    if settings.quantization in [
+        QuantizationMethod.BNB_4BIT,
+        QuantizationMethod.BNB_8BIT,
+    ]:
+        # Quantized models need special handling - we must reload the base model
+        # in full precision to merge the LoRA adapters
+        print()
+        print(
+            "[yellow]Model was loaded with quantization. Merging requires reloading the base model.[/]"
+        )
+        print(
+            "[red](!) WARNING: CPU Merging requires dequantizing the entire model to System RAM.[/]"
+        )
+        print("[red]    This can lead to SYSTEM FREEZES if you run out of memory.[/]")
+        print(
+            "[yellow]    Rule of thumb: You need approx. 3x the parameter count in GB.[/]"
+        )
+        print(
+            "[yellow]    Example: A 27B model requires ~80GB RAM. A 70B model requires ~200GB RAM.[/]"
+        )
+        print()
+
+        merge_choice = prompt_select(
+            "How do you want to proceed?",
+            choices=[
+                Choice(
+                    title="Merge full model (reload base model on CPU - requires high RAM)",
+                    value="merge",
+                ),
+                Choice(
+                    title="Save LoRA adapter only (can be merged later with llama.cpp or more RAM)",
+                    value="adapter",
+                ),
+            ],
+        )
+
+        if merge_choice is None or merge_choice == "adapter":

+    Returns None if user chooses to save LoRA adapter only.
+    """
+    if not isinstance(model.model, PeftModel):
+        # No LoRA adapters, return the model as-is
+        return model.model
+
+    # Prompt for all PEFT models to ensure user is aware of merge implications
+    if settings.quantization in [
+        QuantizationMethod.BNB_4BIT,
+        QuantizationMethod.BNB_8BIT,
+    ]:
+        # Quantized models need special handling - we must reload the base model
+        # in full precision to merge the LoRA adapters
+        print()
+        print(
+            "[yellow]Model was loaded with quantization. Merging requires reloading the base model.[/]"
+        )
+        print(
+            "[red](!) WARNING: CPU Merging requires dequantizing the entire model to System RAM.[/]"
+        )
+        print("[red]    This can lead to SYSTEM FREEZES if you run out of memory.[/]")
+        print(
+            "[yellow]    Rule of thumb: You need approx. 3x the parameter count in GB.[/]"
+        )
+        print(
+            "[yellow]    Example: A 27B model requires ~80GB RAM. A 70B model requires ~200GB RAM.[/]"
+        )
+        print()
+
+        merge_choice = prompt_select(
+            "How do you want to proceed?",
+            choices=[
+                Choice(
+                    title="Merge full model (reload base model on CPU - requires high RAM)",
+                    value="merge",
+                ),
+                Choice(
+                    title="Save LoRA adapter only (can be merged later with llama.cpp or more RAM)",
+                    value="adapter",
+                ),
+            ],
+        )
+
+        if merge_choice is None or merge_choice == "adapter":
discussion_comments.comment_body: If `merge_choice` is `None`, the user pressed Ctrl+C, and I don't think we should do anything in that case. Cancelling should not be equivalent to making one of the choices.

-----
 
 """
+
+
+def is_notebook() -> bool:
+    import os
+    import sys
+
+    # 1. Check for Google Colab environment variables
+    if "COLAB_GPU" in os.environ or "COLAB_RELEASE_TAG" in os.environ:
+        return True
+
+    # 2. Check for Google Colab module
+    if "google.colab" in sys.modules:
+        return True
+
+    # 3. Check for Kaggle environment variables
+    if (
+        "KAGGLE_KERNEL_RUN_TYPE" in os.environ
+        or "KAGGLE_DATA_PROXY_TOKEN" in os.environ
+    ):
+        return True
+
+    # 4. Check IPython shell type
+    try:
+        from IPython import get_ipython
+
+        shell = get_ipython()
+        if shell is None:
+            return False
+
+        shell_name = shell.__class__.__name__
+        # Standard Jupyter/Colab shells
+        if shell_name in ["ZMQInteractiveShell", "Shell"]:
+            return True
+
+        # Some other environments might have different shell names but still be notebooks
+        if "google.colab" in str(shell.__class__):
+            return True
+
+    except (ImportError, NameError, AttributeError):
+        return False
+
+    return False
+
+
+def prompt_select(message: str, choices: list, style=None):
+    if is_notebook():
+        print()
+        print(message)
+        real_choices = []
+        for i, choice in enumerate(choices, 1):
+            if isinstance(choice, Choice):
+                print(f"[{i}] {choice.title}")
+                real_choices.append(choice.value)
+            else:
+                print(f"[{i}] {choice}")
+                real_choices.append(choice)
+
+        print()
+        while True:
+            print(f"Enter number (1-{len(real_choices)}): ", end="")
+            selection = input()
+            if not selection:
+                return None
+            if selection.isdigit() and 1 <= int(selection) <= len(real_choices):
+                return real_choices[int(selection) - 1]
+            print("Please enter a valid number")
+    else:
+        return questionary.select(message, choices=choices, style=style).ask()
+
+
+def prompt_text(message: str, default: str = None, qmark: str = "?") -> str:
+    if is_notebook():
+        prompt = f"{message} [{default}]: " if default else f"{message}: "
+        if qmark == ">":  # Chat mode
+            prompt = "User: "
+
+        print(prompt, end="")
+        value = input()
+
+        # Handle exit commands in notebook chat mode
+        if qmark == ">" and value.strip().lower() in ["/exit", "exit", "quit"]:

 """
+
+
+def is_notebook() -> bool:
+    import os
+    import sys
+
+    # 1. Check for Google Colab environment variables
+    if "COLAB_GPU" in os.environ or "COLAB_RELEASE_TAG" in os.environ:
+        return True
+
+    # 2. Check for Google Colab module
+    if "google.colab" in sys.modules:
+        return True
+
+    # 3. Check for Kaggle environment variables
+    if (
+        "KAGGLE_KERNEL_RUN_TYPE" in os.environ
+        or "KAGGLE_DATA_PROXY_TOKEN" in os.environ
+    ):
+        return True
+
+    # 4. Check IPython shell type
+    try:
+        from IPython import get_ipython
+
+        shell = get_ipython()
+        if shell is None:
+            return False
+
+        shell_name = shell.__class__.__name__
+        # Standard Jupyter/Colab shells
+        if shell_name in ["ZMQInteractiveShell", "Shell"]:
+            return True
+
+        # Some other environments might have different shell names but still be notebooks
+        if "google.colab" in str(shell.__class__):
+            return True
+
+    except (ImportError, NameError, AttributeError):
+        return False
+
+    return False
+
+
+def prompt_select(message: str, choices: list, style=None):
+    if is_notebook():
+        print()
+        print(message)
+        real_choices = []
+        for i, choice in enumerate(choices, 1):
+            if isinstance(choice, Choice):
+                print(f"[{i}] {choice.title}")
+                real_choices.append(choice.value)
+            else:
+                print(f"[{i}] {choice}")
+                real_choices.append(choice)
+
+        print()
+        while True:
+            print(f"Enter number (1-{len(real_choices)}): ", end="")
+            selection = input()
+            if not selection:
+                return None
+            if selection.isdigit() and 1 <= int(selection) <= len(real_choices):
+                return real_choices[int(selection) - 1]
+            print("Please enter a valid number")
+    else:
+        return questionary.select(message, choices=choices, style=style).ask()
+
+
+def prompt_text(message: str, default: str = None, qmark: str = "?") -> str:
+    if is_notebook():
+        prompt = f"{message} [{default}]: " if default else f"{message}: "
+        if qmark == ">":  # Chat mode
+            prompt = "User: "

+

discussion_comments.comment_body:
The chat loop exits when an empty string is entered. I don't think those magic values are necessary.

Relying on the `qmark` as a differentiator is also very brittle.
